docs.text: If two objects have different hashCode but equals() returns true, it breaks the equals-hashCode contract. HashSet may store both objects in different buckets, causing duplicates.

docs.title: 🔥 Super Important Interview One-Liner
docs.text: HashSet uniqueness is guaranteed only when equals() and hashCode() follow the contract.

docs.title: Quick check (yes/no)
docs.text: If you override equals() in a custom class, must you override hashCode() also? Why?

docs.title: ✅ Correct Interview Answer
docs.text: Yes. Whenever we override equals(), we must also override hashCode(). Because HashSet and HashMap use hashCode() to determine the bucket and equals() to check equality within that bucket. If we override only equals() and not hashCode(), equal objects may go into different buckets, breaking uniqueness and causing inconsistent behavior.

docs.title: 🔥 Why This Is Critical (Deep Understanding)
docs.text:
HashMap insertion steps:

1️⃣ Calculate hashCode() 2️⃣ Find bucket index 3️⃣ Compare with existing elements using equals()

If two objects are logically equal but have different hash codes:

  • They go into different buckets
  • equals() never gets called
  • Both objects get stored
  • HashSet allows duplicates

docs.title: 🔥 Official Contract (Must Memorize)
docs.text:
If:

a.equals(b) == true

Then:

a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()

Must be true.

But reverse is not required:

  • Same hashCode does NOT mean equals() must be true.

docs.title: 🚀 Now We Move to HashMap Deep Internal Working (Core Interview Topic)
docs.text: We are now entering the most important data structure in Java interviews.

docs.title: 🔹 HashMap Internal Structure (Java 8+)
docs.text:
Internally:

Node<K,V>[] table;

Each bucket contains:

static class Node<K,V> {
    final int hash;
    final K key;
    V value;
    Node<K,V> next;
}

So structure is:

Array of buckets
Each bucket → LinkedList OR Tree

docs.title: 🔥 Step-by-Step: What Happens When You Do
docs.text: map.put(key, value);

docs.title: Step 1️⃣ Compute hash
docs.text: int hash = hash(key);

docs.title: Step 2️⃣ Find index
docs.text:
index = (n - 1) & hash;

(n = table length)

docs.title: Step 3️⃣ Check bucket
docs.text:
Case A: bucket empty → Insert new node.

Case B: bucket not empty → Traverse:

  • If key equal → replace value
  • Else → collision → add node

docs.title: Step 4️⃣ Resize Check
docs.text:
If:

size > capacity * loadFactor

Default:

  • capacity = 16
  • loadFactor = 0.75

Resize occurs.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Question
docs.text:
If initial capacity = 16 loadFactor = 0.75

At what size does resize occur?

Correct answer: Resize happens when size becomes 13 (i.e., after inserting the 13th element).

Let’s calculate properly 👇

docs.title: Given
docs.text:
• Initial capacity = 16
  • Load factor = 0.75

Resize threshold formula:

threshold = capacity × loadFactor

So:

16 × 0.75 = 12

Threshold = 12

docs.title: When Does Resize Actually Trigger?
docs.text:
Resize happens when:

size > threshold

So:

  • After inserting 12 elements → size = 12 → OK
  • When inserting 13th element → size becomes 13 → 13 > 12 → resize

docs.title: ✅ Final Answer
docs.text: Threshold = 12 Resize happens when size becomes 13

docs.title: 🔥 Important Interview Detail
docs.text:
Many people say:

Resize happens at 12.

That is incorrect.

Correct statement:

Resize happens when inserting the 13th element.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Slightly Harder
docs.text:
After resize, what will be new capacity?

Initial capacity was 16.

Answer?

✅ Answer — correct instinct. It becomes double.

docs.title: 🔥 After Resize
docs.text:
Initial:

  • Capacity = 16
  • Threshold = 12

When inserting 13th element → resize triggered.

New capacity:

newCapacity = oldCapacity * 2

So:

16 → 32

docs.title: 🔥 What about new threshold?
docs.text:
Threshold = capacity × loadFactor

32 × 0.75 = 24

So after resize:

  • Capacity = 32
  • Threshold = 24

Resize will happen again when size becomes 25.

docs.title: 🧠 Important Difference from ArrayList
docs.text:
Structure Resize Factor ArrayList 1.5x HashMap 2x

HashMap doubles capacity to reduce collisions.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Concept (Senior-Level)
docs.text:
When HashMap resizes, does it:

A) Just copy buckets to new array B) Recalculate hash and redistribute entries

✅ Answer — it redistributes entries (rehashes to new buckets). But small refinement: it usually doesn’t “recalculate hashCode()” of the key again; it recomputes bucket index using the stored hash and the new table size.

docs.title: What really happens on HashMap resize (deep)
docs.text:
When capacity doubles (e.g., 16 → 32):

  • HashMap creates a new Node[] table of size 32
  • For each existing entry, it decides the new bucket using:
newIndex = hash & (newCapacity - 1)

Since newCapacity changed, bucket index changes, so entries move.

docs.title: Java 8 optimization (interview bonus)
docs.text:
During resize, Java 8 doesn’t fully recompute everything expensively. It uses a smart trick:

When capacity doubles, each entry either:

  • stays in the same index, or
  • moves to index + oldCapacity

Based on one bit:

(hash & oldCapacity) == 0  → stay
(hash & oldCapacity) != 0  → move to index + oldCapacity

This makes resizing faster.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview one-liner
docs.text: On resize, HashMap redistributes nodes into a larger table because bucket index depends on capacity. In Java 8, entries either stay or move by oldCapacity based on one hash bit.

docs.title: 🔥 Next Critical Question (Collision handling)
docs.text:
In Java 8+, when many keys collide into the same bucket, HashMap may convert the bucket from a LinkedList into a Tree.

  1. What is this conversion called?
  2. After how many nodes (default) does it happen?

docs.title: 1️⃣ Conversion Name
docs.text:
✔ Treeification

When a bucket (collision chain) becomes too long, LinkedList → converted to Red-Black Tree

docs.title: 2️⃣ When does it happen?
docs.text:
✔ When bucket size becomes ≥ 8

More precisely:

  • If bucket size > TREEIFY_THRESHOLD (8)
  • AND table size ≥ 64

Only then treeification happens.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Condition (Many Miss This)
docs.text:
Treeification happens only if:

bucket size ≥ 8
AND
table capacity ≥ 64

If capacity < 64:

  • HashMap will resize instead of treeify.

This avoids unnecessary tree creation.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: In Java 8+, if a bucket contains more than 8 nodes and table size is at least 64, it is converted into a Red-Black Tree to improve lookup from O(n) to O(log n).

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Question
docs.text:
What is the time complexity of:

1️⃣ HashMap get() normally 2️⃣ HashMap get() in worst case (before Java 8) 3️⃣ HashMap get() in worst case (after treeification)

✅ Answers:

docs.title: HashMap get() Complexity (Interview-Ready)
docs.text:
1️⃣ Normal average case: O(1)

  • Good hash distribution, small bucket size.

2️⃣ Worst case before Java 8: O(n)

  • All keys collide → one long linked list bucket → linear search.

3️⃣ Worst case after treeification (Java 8+): O(log n)

  • Long bucket becomes Red-Black Tree → log-time lookup.

docs.title: 🔥 Ultra Important Interview Trap: null in HashMap
docs.text:
Answer these:

  1. Does HashMap allow null key?
  2. Does HashMap allow multiple null keys?
  3. How many null keys allowed?
  4. Does HashMap allow null values?

Now let’s go deep because this is a very common interview trap.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Does HashMap allow null key?
docs.text:
✔ Yes

HashMap allows one null key.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Does HashMap allow multiple null keys?
docs.text:
❌ No

Because:

  • Keys must be unique
  • Only one null key allowed

If you insert:

map.put(null, 10);
map.put(null, 20);

Second one replaces the first.

docs.title: 3️⃣ How many null keys allowed?
docs.text: ✔ Only 1

docs.title: 4️⃣ Does HashMap allow null values?
docs.text:
✔ Yes

Multiple null values allowed.

Example:

map.put("A", null);
map.put("B", null);

Perfectly valid.

docs.title: 🔥 Now the Important Internal Question
docs.text:
Where does HashMap store null key internally?

Think carefully.

Since null has no hashCode, what bucket is used?

✅ Answer — it is stored in the first bucket (index 0).

Now let’s explain properly and deeply.

docs.title: 🔥 How HashMap Handles null Key Internally
docs.text:
When you do:

map.put(null, "value");

HashMap logic:

if (key == null)
    return putForNullKey(value);

It does NOT call hashCode().

Instead:

  • It directly inserts into bucket index 0

Why?

Because:

hash(null) = 0
index = (n - 1) & 0 = 0

So null key always goes to bucket 0.

docs.title: 🧠 Important Interview Insight
docs.text: HashMap allows one null key and stores it in bucket 0.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Hard Question (Very Important)
docs.text: Why does HashMap allow null key but Hashtable does NOT?

docs.title: 🔥 Why HashMap allows null but Hashtable does NOT
1️⃣ Design Difference
docs.text:
HashMap

  • Introduced in Java 1.2
  • Not synchronized
  • More flexible
  • Allows 1 null key and multiple null values

Hashtable

  • Legacy class (Java 1.0)
  • Fully synchronized
  • Does NOT allow null key or null value

docs.title: 🔥 The Real Reason (Important)
docs.text:
Hashtable methods are synchronized and older design assumed:

value = table[index];
if (value == null)
    // means key not present

If null values were allowed:

  • Cannot distinguish between:

  • Key not present

  • Key present but value = null

So to avoid ambiguity:

Hashtable does not allow null key or null value.

docs.title: 🔥 Why HashMap allows null then?
docs.text:
HashMap is more modern and internally handles null carefully:

  • Special handling for null key (bucket 0)
  • Null values allowed
  • Uses containsKey() to differentiate

Example:

map.put("A", null);

map.get("A") → null
map.containsKey("A") → true

So HashMap can differentiate.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Hashtable does not allow null keys or values to avoid ambiguity and because of its legacy synchronized design, while HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Question
docs.text:
Which is thread-safe?

1️⃣ HashMap 2️⃣ Hashtable 3️⃣ ConcurrentHashMap

Correct answer:

docs.title: 3️⃣ ConcurrentHashMap → ✅ Thread-safe (better design)
docs.text: Now let’s explain properly.

docs.title: 🔥 1️⃣ HashMap
docs.text:
• Not synchronized
  • If multiple threads modify it → data corruption possible
  • Can even cause infinite loop during resize (older Java versions)

So:

HashMap → NOT thread-safe

docs.title: 🔥 2️⃣ Hashtable
docs.text:
• All methods are synchronized
  • Only one thread can access at a time
  • Safe but slow

Example:

public synchronized V put(K key, V value)

So:

Hashtable → Thread-safe but inefficient

docs.title: 🔥 3️⃣ ConcurrentHashMap (Most Important)
docs.text:
• Designed for concurrency
  • Not fully synchronized
  • Uses fine-grained locking
  • Allows multiple threads to read/write safely
  • Much faster than Hashtable

So:

ConcurrentHashMap → Thread-safe and efficient

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: HashMap is not thread-safe. Hashtable is thread-safe but fully synchronized and slow. ConcurrentHashMap is thread-safe with better performance using segmented locking (Java 7) or CAS + synchronized bins (Java 8+).

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Question
docs.text:
Does ConcurrentHashMap allow null key or null value?

Think carefully. This is tricky.

✅ Answer — ConcurrentHashMap does NOT allow null key or null value.

Now let’s understand why (very important for interviews).

docs.title: 🔥 Why ConcurrentHashMap does NOT allow null?
docs.text:
In concurrent environment:

If this was allowed:

map.put("A", null);

Now if another thread does:

map.get("A");

It returns null.

Now ambiguity:

  • Is key not present?
  • Or key present but value is null?

In single-threaded HashMap, you can call:

map.containsKey("A");

But in concurrent environment:

Between get() and containsKey():

  • Another thread may remove or modify entry.
  • So result becomes inconsistent.

To avoid this ambiguity:

ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null keys or values.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null keys or values because in concurrent environments null creates ambiguity between “key absent” and “value null”.

docs.title: 🔥 Quick Comparison Table
docs.text: Map Type Thread-Safe Null Key Null Value HashMap ❌ No ✅ 1 ✅ Yes Hashtable ✅ Yes ❌ No ❌ No ConcurrentHashMap ✅ Yes ❌ No ❌ No

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Concept: Fail-Fast
docs.text:
What happens if you modify an ArrayList while iterating using for-each loop?

Example:

ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add(1);
list.add(2);
list.add(3);

for (Integer i : list) {
    list.remove(i);
}

What happens?

✅ Answer: — it throws:

ConcurrentModificationException

Now let’s go deep and make it interview-ready.

docs.title: Why ConcurrentModificationException Happens?
docs.text:
When you iterate using:

for (Integer i : list)

Internally it uses an Iterator:

Iterator<Integer> it = list.iterator();

ArrayList maintains:

modCount

Every structural modification:

  • add()
  • remove()
  • clear()

Increments modCount.

docs.title: What Iterator Stores
docs.text:
Iterator stores:

expectedModCount

During iteration:

if (modCount != expectedModCount)
    throw ConcurrentModificationException;

So when you modify list directly:

list.remove(i);

modCount changes But iterator’s expectedModCount does not → mismatch → exception thrown

docs.title: 🔥 Important Interview Concept
docs.text:
This is called:

Fail-Fast behavior

Fail-fast means:

  • Detects structural modification
  • Fails immediately
  • Does not allow unpredictable behavior

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: ArrayList iterators are fail-fast. If the collection is structurally modified after the iterator is created (except through iterator.remove()), it throws ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Question
docs.text: How can you safely remove elements while iterating?

docs.title: ✅ Correct & Recommended Approach (Interview Answer)
docs.text:
Use Iterator.remove()

Iterator<Integer> it = list.iterator();
while (it.hasNext()) {
    Integer val = it.next();
    if (val % 2 == 0) {
        it.remove();  // SAFE
    }
}

Why safe?

Because:

  • it.remove() updates both

  • modCount

  • expectedModCount
  • So no mismatch

docs.title: 🔥 Java 8+ Best Way
docs.text:
Use:

list.removeIf(val -> val % 2 == 0);

Internally safe.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: To safely remove elements while iterating, use Iterator.remove() or removeIf(). Modifying the collection directly during iteration causes ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Question
docs.text:
Are HashMap iterators fail-fast?

Example:

for (String key : map.keySet()) {
    map.remove(key);
}

Will this throw exception?

✅ Yes, it will throw ConcurrentModificationException (in normal HashMap).

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
HashMap iterators are also fail-fast, just like ArrayList.

When you iterate:

for (String key : map.keySet())

Internally it uses an iterator over the keySet view, and HashMap also maintains a modification count (modCount).

If you do:

map.remove(key);

That’s a structural modification while iterating → modCount changes → iterator detects mismatch → throws ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: ✅ Correct ways to remove while iterating a HashMap
1️⃣ Use iterator.remove()
docs.text:
Iterator<String> it = map.keySet().iterator();
while (it.hasNext()) {
    String key = it.next();
    if (/* condition */) {
        it.remove(); // safe
    }
}

docs.title: 2️⃣ Use removeIf on entrySet (Java 8+)
docs.text: map.entrySet().removeIf(e -> /* condition */);

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: HashMap iterators are fail-fast. Modifying the map structurally during iteration (except via iterator.remove()) causes ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: 🔥 Next: TreeSet / TreeMap Core Concept
docs.text:
Before we move there, answer this:

What is the difference between HashMap and TreeMap in terms of ordering and internal data structure? (2–3 lines)

HashMap not maintain sorting order but TreeMap does. Tree map maintains sorted order (based on natural ordering or Comparator). May be HashMap internally use arrays to store bucket and TreeMap use tree data structure.

Now let’s make it interview-perfect.

docs.title: HashMap
docs.text:
• Does NOT maintain insertion order
  • Does NOT maintain sorted order
  • Order is unpredictable

docs.title: TreeMap
docs.text:
• Maintains sorted order

  • Sorting based on:

  • Natural ordering (Comparable)

  • OR custom Comparator

docs.title: HashMap
docs.text:
• Uses:

  • Array of buckets

  • LinkedList (before Java 8)
  • Red-Black Tree (if bucket size ≥ 8)

So it's primarily hash-based.

docs.title: TreeMap
docs.text:
• Uses Red-Black Tree
  • Self-balancing binary search tree
  • All operations O(log n)

docs.title: 🔥 Time Complexity Comparison
docs.text: Operation HashMap TreeMap get() O(1) average O(log n) put() O(1) average O(log n) remove() O(1) average O(log n) Ordering No Sorted

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: HashMap is hash-based and provides O(1) average time complexity without ordering guarantees. TreeMap is a Red-Black Tree that maintains sorted order with O(log n) operations.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Concept
docs.text:
TreeMap requires keys to be:

1️⃣ Comparable OR 2️⃣ Provided with Comparator

Why?

What happens if keys are not comparable?

Correct answer:

👉 It will throw ClassCastException at runtime.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
TreeMap uses a Red-Black Tree.

To insert a key, it must compare keys:

Either using:

Comparable.compareTo()

OR

Comparator.compare()

If keys are not comparable, TreeMap cannot determine:

  • left child
  • right child

So it fails during insertion.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Person {
    int id;
}

TreeMap<Person, String> map = new TreeMap<>();
map.put(new Person(), "A");

This will throw:

java.lang.ClassCastException

Because Person does not implement Comparable and no Comparator provided.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: TreeMap requires keys to be comparable. If keys are not comparable and no Comparator is provided, it throws ClassCastException at runtime.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Deep Question
docs.text:
What is difference between:

1️⃣ Comparable 2️⃣ Comparator

Explain in 3–4 lines. This is very common interview question.

Comparable: It is a interface provide a compare method to decide natural sort order.
Comparator you can use explicity to sort the collection.

Now let’s make it interview-perfect + deeper.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Comparable
docs.text:
• Package: java.lang
  • Method:
int compareTo(T o);
  • Defines natural ordering
  • Class itself implements it
  • Only one natural order possible

Example:

class Person implements Comparable<Person> {
    int age;

    public int compareTo(Person p) {
        return this.age - p.age;
    }
}

Now:

Collections.sort(list);

Uses compareTo()

docs.title: 2️⃣ Comparator
docs.text:
• Package: java.util
  • Method:
int compare(T o1, T o2);
  • Used for custom ordering
  • Separate class or lambda
  • Multiple sorting strategies possible

Example:

Comparator<Person> byName = (p1, p2) -> p1.name.compareTo(p2.name);
Collections.sort(list, byName);

docs.title: 🔥 Key Differences Table
docs.text: Feature Comparable Comparator Package java.lang java.util Method compareTo() compare() Defined in Same class Separate class Natural order Yes No Multiple sorting ❌ Only one ✅ Multiple

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Comparable defines the natural ordering of objects within the class, while Comparator defines custom ordering externally and allows multiple sorting strategies.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Edge Case
docs.text:
If compareTo() returns 0, what does that mean in:

1️⃣ TreeSet 2️⃣ TreeMap

Think carefully — this is important.

Correct answer:

If compareTo() returns 0, it means:

The two objects are considered equal in terms of ordering.

And in TreeSet / TreeMap, that means:

👉 The second element will NOT be inserted.

docs.title: 🔥 Why?
docs.text:
TreeSet and TreeMap use:

  • compareTo() (or Comparator)
  • NOT equals() to determine uniqueness

So if:

obj1.compareTo(obj2) == 0

TreeSet treats them as duplicates — even if equals() returns false.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Person implements Comparable<Person> {
    int age;
    String name;

    public int compareTo(Person p) {
        return this.age - p.age;  // only age considered
    }
}

Now:

TreeSet<Person> set = new TreeSet<>();

set.add(new Person(25, "A"));
set.add(new Person(25, "B"));

Since compareTo returns 0 (same age):

Second object is NOT added.

Even though names differ.

docs.title: 🧠 Critical Interview Rule
docs.text: In TreeSet and TreeMap, uniqueness is determined by compareTo() (or Comparator), not equals().

docs.title: 🚀 Now Deep Question
docs.text:
If equals() says two objects are equal, but compareTo() does NOT return 0,

what happens in TreeSet?

✅ Answer:

If:

  • equals() returns true
  • but compareTo() does NOT return 0

👉 Both objects will be inserted into TreeSet.

docs.title: 🔥 Why?
docs.text:
Because:

TreeSet uses:

compareTo() OR Comparator

to determine:

  • Order
  • Uniqueness

It does NOT use equals() to prevent duplicates.

So if:

obj1.equals(obj2) == true

But:

obj1.compareTo(obj2) != 0

TreeSet thinks they are different.

docs.title: 🚨 Important Rule (Very Important for Interviews)
docs.text:
For sorted collections:

compareTo() returning 0 defines equality, not equals().

docs.title: 🔥 Why This Is Dangerous?
docs.text:
Because this violates the general contract:

Java recommends:

if a.equals(b) == true
then a.compareTo(b) should return 0

If not:

  • You may get inconsistent behavior
  • Collections behave unexpectedly

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: In TreeSet and TreeMap, equality is determined by compareTo() or Comparator. If compareTo() is inconsistent with equals(), it may allow logical duplicates.

docs.title: 🚀 We Have Covered:
docs.text:
• ArrayList internals
  • LinkedList internals
  • HashSet internals
  • HashMap internals
  • Resize logic
  • Treeification
  • Fail-fast
  • Null behavior
  • TreeMap internals
  • Comparable vs Comparator
  • Uniqueness rules

You are now at strong intermediate-to-senior level on Collections.

docs.title: TOP 14 COLLECTIONS FRAMEWORK 2
docs.text:
Following topics we will conver in this part:

1️⃣ Time Complexity Master Table (Complete Revision Map) 2️⃣ Red-Black Tree Basics (for TreeMap understanding) 3️⃣ ConcurrentHashMap Internals (Deep concurrency insight) 4️⃣ Generics Deep Dive (PECS, Wildcards, Type Erasure)

We start with Phase 1.

docs.title: 🔥 PHASE 1 — MASTER TIME COMPLEXITY TABLE (Interview Gold)
docs.text: You must know this without thinking.

docs.title: 1️⃣ List Implementations
docs.text:
Operation ArrayList LinkedList add(e) O(1)* O(1) add(index, e) O(n) O(n) get(index) O(1) O(n) remove(index) O(n) O(n) removeFirst() O(n) O(1) removeLast() O(1) O(1) contains() O(n) O(n) Iteration O(n) O(n)
  • O(1) amortized.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Set Implementations
docs.text:
Operation HashSet LinkedHashSet TreeSet add() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) remove() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) contains() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) Ordering No Insertion Sorted Null allowed Yes Yes No (if using natural ordering)
  • average case.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Map Implementations
docs.text: Operation HashMap LinkedHashMap TreeMap ConcurrentHashMap put() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) O(1)* get() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) O(1)* remove() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) O(1)* Ordering No Insertion Sorted No Thread-safe No No No Yes Null key 1 1 No No

docs.title: 4️⃣ Worst Case Summary
docs.text: Structure Worst Case HashMap (pre-Java 8) O(n) HashMap (Java 8+) O(log n) TreeMap O(log n) ArrayList add O(n) (resize) LinkedList get O(n)

docs.title: 🔥 Important Pattern Recognition
docs.text:
Hash-based structures:

  • Fast average
  • Depend on hashCode quality

Tree-based structures:

  • Always O(log n)
  • Ordered

Array-based structures:

  • Fast random access
  • Expensive shifting

Linked structures:

  • Fast head/tail ops
  • Slow random access

docs.title: 🚀 Now Small Rapid-Fire Test
docs.text:
Answer without thinking too much:

1️⃣ Fastest for search? (Average case) 2️⃣ Best for sorted data? 3️⃣ Best for LRU cache base? 4️⃣ Best for concurrent high-read workload? 5️⃣ Best for frequent head insertions?

Answer 1 line each.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Fastest for search (average case)?
docs.text: ✔ Hash-based structures (HashMap / HashSet) Because average time complexity is O(1).

docs.title: 2️⃣ Best for sorted data?
docs.text: ✔ TreeMap / TreeSet Because they maintain sorted order using Red-Black Tree.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Best for LRU cache base?
docs.text:
You said: “Maybe HashBased” → ❌ Not precise.

✔ Correct answer: LinkedHashMap

Why?

LinkedHashMap:

  • Maintains insertion order
  • Can maintain access order
  • Supports overriding:
protected boolean removeEldestEntry()

Used internally to build LRU cache.

Interview one-liner:

LinkedHashMap is best for implementing LRU cache because it maintains access order.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Best for concurrent high-read workload?
docs.text:
✔ ConcurrentHashMap

Because:

  • Thread-safe
  • Allows high concurrency
  • Uses fine-grained locking / CAS

docs.title: 5️⃣ Best for frequent head insertions?
docs.text:
✔ LinkedList

Because:

  • O(1) insertion at head
  • No shifting

docs.title: 🔥 Summary: You’re thinking in correct direction.
docs.text:
Only LRU needed correction.

Now we go deeper.

docs.title: 🚀 PHASE 2 — RED-BLACK TREE BASICS
docs.text:
This is important for:

  • TreeMap
  • TreeSet
  • HashMap treeification

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is Red-Black Tree?
docs.text:
It is a:

Self-balancing Binary Search Tree

Guarantees:

Height ≈ log n

So operations:

insert → O(log n)
delete → O(log n)
search → O(log n)

docs.title: 2️⃣ Why Not Normal BST?
docs.text:
Normal BST can become:

1
 \
  2
   \
    3
     \
      4

Height becomes n Operations become O(n)

Red-Black Tree prevents that.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Core Red-Black Rules (Memorize)
docs.text:
1️⃣ Every node is either RED or BLACK 2️⃣ Root is always BLACK 3️⃣ No two RED nodes adjacent 4️⃣ Every path from node to null has same number of BLACK nodes 5️⃣ Null leaves are BLACK

These rules maintain balance.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Why TreeMap Uses Red-Black Tree?
docs.text:
Because:

  • Predictable O(log n)
  • Self-balancing
  • Efficient for sorted maps

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Insight
docs.text:
When HashMap bucket size ≥ 8:

  • LinkedList → converted to Red-Black Tree
  • Improves from O(n) → O(log n)

docs.title: ✅ Which is faster for small data size?
docs.text: ✔ HashMap

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
• Average time complexity = O(1)
  • No tree balancing overhead
  • Lower constant factors

TreeMap:

  • Always O(log n)
  • Has Red-Black Tree rotations
  • More pointer operations

So for small and unsorted data:

HashMap is generally faster.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Insight
docs.text:
Even though HashMap worst-case is O(log n) (Java 8+), TreeMap always remains O(log n).

So unless you need sorted order:

Prefer HashMap.

docs.title: 🚀 Now PHASE 3 — ConcurrentHashMap Deep Internals
docs.text: This is advanced and very important for backend interviews.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Why Not Just Use HashMap in Multi-thread?
docs.text:
If multiple threads modify HashMap:

  • Data corruption
  • Lost updates
  • Infinite loop during resize (pre-Java 8)
  • Race conditions

So not safe.

docs.title: 2️⃣ How Hashtable Solves It?
docs.text:
• Entire methods synchronized

Example:

public synchronized V put(K key, V value)

Problem:

  • Only one thread at a time
  • Low performance

docs.title: Java 7 approach:
docs.text:
• Segmented locking
  • Divides map into segments
  • Lock only segment

docs.title: Java 8+ approach:
docs.text:
• No segments

  • Uses:

  • CAS (Compare-And-Swap)

  • synchronized only on bucket level

So:

  • Multiple threads can operate on different buckets simultaneously

docs.title: 🔥 Important Differences
docs.text: Feature HashMap ConcurrentHashMap Thread-safe No Yes Locking None Fine-grained Null allowed Yes No Resize safe No Yes

docs.title: 🔥 Key Concept: CAS
docs.text:
CAS = Compare And Swap

Used for:

  • Lock-free updates
  • High concurrency

docs.title: 🚀 Interview-Level Question
docs.text:
Is ConcurrentHashMap completely lock-free?

✅ Answer — No, ConcurrentHashMap is not completely lock-free.

Now let’s explain properly at interview level.

docs.title: 🔥 Is ConcurrentHashMap completely lock-free?
docs.text:
👉 No.

It uses a combination of:

  • CAS (Compare-And-Swap) for some operations
  • Synchronized blocks (on bins/buckets) for others

So it is:

Partially lock-free, but not fully lock-free.

docs.title: 🔍 What is Lock-Free Here?
docs.text:
For example:

  • When inserting into an empty bucket
  • It uses CAS to set the value

No lock needed.

docs.title: 🔒 When Does It Use Lock?
docs.text:
If:

  • Bucket already contains elements
  • Collision happens
  • Tree bin modification

Then it uses:

synchronized (bucketNode) {
   // modify bucket
}

But only that specific bucket is locked, not whole map.

docs.title: 🔥 Why This Is Powerful?
docs.text:
Because:

  • Different threads can modify different buckets simultaneously.
  • Very high concurrency compared to Hashtable.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: ConcurrentHashMap is not fully lock-free. It uses CAS for simple operations and synchronized blocks at bucket level for collision handling, providing high concurrency without full map locking.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Question
docs.text:
Does ConcurrentHashMap allow:

1️⃣ Concurrent reads without locking? 2️⃣ Concurrent writes on different buckets?

docs.title: 1️⃣ Concurrent reads without locking?
docs.text:
✔ Yes

ConcurrentHashMap allows:

  • Multiple threads to read simultaneously
  • No locking required for read operations (in most cases)

Why?

  • It uses volatile variables
  • Proper memory visibility guarantees
  • Reads do not block writes

docs.title: 2️⃣ Concurrent writes on different buckets?
docs.text:
✔ Yes

Because:

  • Locking happens at bucket level (Java 8+)
  • Different threads modifying different buckets
  • No interference

This gives high concurrency.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Clarification
docs.text:
If two threads modify the same bucket:

  • Then synchronization happens
  • Only one thread modifies that bucket at a time

But other buckets remain free.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and concurrent writes on different buckets using fine-grained locking and CAS, improving performance over Hashtable.

docs.title: 🚀 Now PHASE 4 — GENERICS (Deep + Senior-Level)
docs.text: This is extremely important.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Why Generics?
docs.text:
Before Java 5:

```java id="zpmv0t" List list = new ArrayList(); list.add("A"); list.add(10);


Problem:

* No type safety
* ClassCastException at runtime

Generics introduced:

* Compile-time type safety
* No casting needed

---

# 2️⃣ Basic Syntax

```java id="1k0msp"
List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();

Now:

  • Only String allowed
  • Compile-time safety

docs.title: 3️⃣ What is Type Erasure?
docs.text:
Generics exist only at compile-time.

At runtime:

```java id="h5bivv" List 


Becomes:

```java id="cjj3cn"
List

Type information erased.

So:

List<String>.class

Does NOT exist.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Consequence
docs.text:
You cannot do:

```java id="xok5qq" if (obj instanceof List) 


Because generics erased at runtime.

---

# 🚀 Now Important Question

Can you create:

```java id="sdntm1"
List<int> list = new ArrayList<>();

Why or why not?

✅ Answer: No, we can not create using primitive types.

> Generics work only with reference types because of type erasure. Primitive types like int must be wrapped in their corresponding wrapper classes like Integer.

Small correction:

  • Wrapper classes like Integer do not extend Object directly, they extend Number, and Number extends Object.

Now let’s give the perfect answer.

docs.title: ✅ Why List<int> is Not Allowed?
docs.text:
List<int> list = new ArrayList<>(); ❌

Because:

1️⃣ Generics work only with reference types 2️⃣ int is a primitive type, not a reference type 3️⃣ Generics use type erasure, and primitives cannot be used in type parameters

So you must use:

List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();

Java automatically handles autoboxing:

list.add(10);   // int → Integer

docs.title: 🔥 Important Interview Insight
docs.text:
Why generics don’t support primitives?

Because internally after type erasure:

List<Integer>

Becomes:

List

And everything is treated as Object.

But primitive types are not objects.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Generics work only with reference types because of type erasure. Primitive types like int must be wrapped in their corresponding wrapper classes like Integer.

docs.title: 🚀 Now We Go Deeper — Wildcards
docs.text:
Explain the difference between:

List<?>
List<Object>
List<? extends Number>
List<? super Integer>

We go step-by-step.

docs.title: ✅ What is List<?> ?
docs.text:
Correct meaning:

List<?> means a list of unknown type.

It does NOT mean “any reference item allowed to add”.

It means:

  • It could be List<String>
  • Or List<Integer>
  • Or List<Person>
  • We don’t know which.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Rule
docs.text:
With List<?>:

✔ You can read elements as Object ❌ You cannot add elements (except null)

Example:

List<?> list = new ArrayList<String>();

Object obj = list.get(0);   // allowed

list.add("Hello");  // ❌ Compile error
list.add(null);     // ✅ allowed

docs.title: Why can’t we add?
docs.text:
Because compiler does not know actual type.

If:

List<?> list = new ArrayList<Integer>();

Then:

list.add("Hello");  // would break type safety

So Java blocks all additions (except null).

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: List<?> represents a list of unknown type. You can read elements as Object, but you cannot add elements except null.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Next Level
docs.text:
What is the difference between:

List<Object>

and

List<?>

Think carefully. This is very important.

List<?> it define list is any kind of similar type. So basically you don't know the type here. SO you cannot add elements here because type safety.
But List<Object> is define list is object type. It contains object which may or may not similar types.
It will allow add method. Here get method return element as object

Let me refine it into a perfect interview-level explanation.

docs.title: 1️⃣ List<Object>
docs.text:
List<Object> list = new ArrayList<>();

Meaning:

  • This list can store any object type
  • Because everything in Java extends Object

You can:

list.add("Hello");
list.add(10);
list.add(new Person());

✔ You can add elements ✔ get() returns Object

Important:

List<String> is NOT a subtype of List<Object>

This is very important.

List<String> strList = new ArrayList<>();
List<Object> objList = strList;  // ❌ Compile error

Generics are invariant.

docs.title: 2️⃣ List<?>
docs.text:
List<?> list = new ArrayList<String>();

Meaning:

  • A list of unknown type
  • Could be List
  • Could be List
  • Compiler does not know

    • So:

    ✔ You can read as Object ❌ You cannot add elements (except null)

docs.title: 🔥 Key Difference Summary
docs.text: Feature List

docs.title: 1️⃣ Basic Definition (Interview Style)
docs.text:
Type conversion is the process of converting one data type into another. In Java, it happens in two ways:

  • Implicit (Widening)
  • Explicit (Narrowing / Casting)

docs.title: 2️⃣ Implicit Type Conversion (Widening)
docs.text:
Also called: Automatic Casting

When:

  • Smaller type → Larger type
  • No data loss

Example:

int x = 10;
long y = x;   // automatic

docs.title: Why allowed?
docs.text:
Because:

  • long (8 bytes) can hold int (4 bytes)
  • No information lost

docs.title: Primitive Widening Order (Very Important)
docs.text:
byte → short → int → long → float → double
           char ↗

⚠ boolean cannot be converted to any other type.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
byte b = 10;
int i = b;      // OK
double d = i;   // OK

docs.title: 3️⃣ Explicit Type Conversion (Narrowing)
docs.text:
When:

  • Larger type → Smaller type
  • Possible data loss
  • Must use casting operator

Example:

double d = 10.5;
int x = (int) d;

Output:

10

Decimal part removed (not rounded).

docs.title: ⚠️ Data Loss Example
docs.text:
int x = 130;
byte b = (byte) x;
System.out.println(b);

Output:

-126

Why?

Because:

  • byte range: -128 to 127
  • 130 exceeds range
  • Overflow happens
  • Binary truncation

This is very common interview trap.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 1: Arithmetic Promotion
docs.text:
byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
byte c = a + b;  // ERROR

Why error?

Because:

  • a + b automatically promoted to int
  • So result is int
  • Cannot assign int to byte without casting

Correct:

byte c = (byte)(a + b);

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 2: Char + Int
docs.text:
char c = 'A';
int x = c;   // OK

Output:

65

Because char stores Unicode value.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 3: Final Keyword Trick
docs.text:
final int x = 10;
byte b = x;   // OK

Why?

Because x is compile-time constant within range of byte.

But:

int x = 10;
byte b = x;   // ERROR

Even though value same.

Interviewers love this.

docs.title: 5️⃣ Reference Type Casting
docs.text: There are two:

docs.title: Upcasting (Automatic)
docs.text:
Animal a = new Dog();

Child → Parent Safe No cast needed

docs.title: Downcasting (Explicit)
docs.text:
Animal a = new Dog();
Dog d = (Dog) a;

Parent → Child Needs cast May cause ClassCastException

docs.title: ⚠️ Dangerous Trap
docs.text:
Animal a = new Animal();
Dog d = (Dog) a;  // Compiles but runtime exception

docs.title: 6️⃣ How to Answer in Interview
docs.text:
If interviewer asks:

👉 “Explain type casting in Java.”

Answer:

In Java, type conversion happens either automatically (widening) when converting smaller types to larger types, or explicitly (narrowing) when converting larger types to smaller types which may cause data loss. Java also supports reference type casting through upcasting and downcasting.

docs.title: 📘 Notes Summary
docs.text:
• Widening → safe → automatic
  • Narrowing → data loss → manual cast
  • Arithmetic → smaller types become int
  • boolean → no conversion
  • Object casting → runtime type matters

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
int x = 5;
double y = x / 2;
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Step-by-step:
docs.text:
• x is int (5)
  • 2 is int
  • So x / 2 → integer division
  • 5 / 2 = 2 (decimal part discarded)

Then:

  • double y = 2;
  • int 2 is widened to double → 2.0

docs.title: ✅ Output:
docs.text:
2.0

✔ It is integer division (fraction part removed)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
double y = 5 / 2;
System.out.println(y);
  • 5 is int
  • 2 is int
  • division happens BEFORE assignment

So result?

👉 It is still integer division.

So:

5 / 2 = 2 Then 2 → converted to double → 2.0

docs.title: ✅ Output:
docs.text:
2.0

Many beginners say 2.5. That is wrong.

docs.title: If you want 2.5
docs.text:
You must make one operand double:

double y = 5 / 2.0;

or

double y = (double)5 / 2;

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Why does Java promote byte/short to int in arithmetic?

This is important.

docs.title: Reason:
docs.text:
CPU works efficiently with at least 32-bit integers.

So Java promotes:

  • byte
  • short
  • char

to int before arithmetic operations.

This is called Integer Promotion Rule.

Example:

byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
int c = a + b;   // promoted to int

Even though both are byte, addition happens in int.

System.out.println(5 / 2.0);
  • 2.0 is double
  • int 5 is promoted to double
  • double division → 2.5
System.out.println(5.0 / 2);
  • 5.0 is double
  • int 2 promoted to double
  • double division → 2.5
System.out.println(5.0 / 2.0);
  • both double
  • double division → 2.5

docs.title: 🔥 Golden Rule (Write in your notes)
docs.text:
If any one operand is double, the entire expression becomes double.

If both operands are int → integer division.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
System.out.println(5 / 2 * 1.0);
System.out.println(5 * 1.0 / 2);

docs.title: 1️⃣ 5 / 2 * 1.0
docs.text:
Operator precedence:

  • / and * have same precedence
  • evaluated left to right

So:

Step 1:

5 / 2

Both int → integer division → 2

Step 2:

2 * 1.0

Now one operand is double → result becomes double → 2.0

✅ Output:

2.0

docs.title: 2️⃣ 5 * 1.0 / 2
docs.text:
Step 1:

5 * 1.0

int promoted to double → 5.0

Step 2:

5.0 / 2

double division → 2.5

✅ Output:

2.5

docs.title: 🔥 What Interviewer Tests Here
docs.text:
They test:

  • Order of evaluation
  • Type promotion
  • Integer division behavior
  • Expression evaluation left to right

docs.title: 📘 Write This Rule in Notes
docs.text: Arithmetic operators evaluate left to right. Type promotion happens at the time of operation, not at assignment.

docs.title: ✅ Why It Does NOT Compile
docs.text:
byte b = 100;
b = b + 1;

docs.title: Step-by-step:
docs.text:
• b is byte
  • 1 is int (default literal type)
  • In arithmetic, byte is promoted to int
  • So b + 1 becomes int
  • Cannot assign int to byte without casting

So compiler error:

possible lossy conversion from int to byte

docs.title: ✅ Correct Version
docs.text:
b = (byte) (b + 1);

Now it compiles.

docs.title: 🔥 Why Does This Compile?
docs.text:
byte b = 100;
b += 1;  // ✅ compiles!

Even though b = b + 1; does NOT compile, this b += 1; version DOES compile.

docs.title: ✅ Reason:
docs.text:
Java applies implicit cast in compound assignment (+=, -=, *=, etc.)

The compiler automatically inserts a cast, like:

b = (byte)(b + 1);

So:

"compiler resolves b += 1 as b = (byte)(b + 1);" ✅

docs.title: ⚠ Why Does This Rule Exist?
docs.text:
To make code cleaner:

  • Compound operators (+=, -=) were designed to allow implicit narrowing cast
  • But direct assignment (b = b + 1;) does not allow implicit cast
  • This is how Java maintains strict typing and convenient shorthand at the same time

docs.title: 📘 Write in Your Notes
docs.text:
byte b = 100;
b = b + 1;       // ❌ Compilation error (int → byte)
b += 1;          // ✅ Compiles (implicit cast)

Same applies to:

short s = 10;
s = s * 2;      // ❌
s *= 2;         // ✅

docs.title: 📘 Operators in Java
docs.text:
We will cover:

  1. Increment / Decrement (++, --)
  2. Short-circuit operators (&&, ||)
  3. Ternary operator (?:)
  4. Bitwise operators (&, |, ^, ~, <<, >>, >>>)

Step by step. No confusion.

docs.title: Two Types
docs.text:
• Pre-increment → ++x
  • Post-increment → x++

docs.title: Post-increment
docs.text:
int x = 5;
int y = x++;

Step-by-step:

  • y = 5
  • then x becomes 6

Final:

x = 6
y = 5

docs.title: Pre-increment
docs.text:
int x = 5;
int y = ++x;

Step-by-step:

  • x becomes 6
  • y = 6

Final:

x = 6
y = 6

docs.title: 🔥 Dangerous Interview Trap
docs.text:
Predict:

int x = 5;
System.out.println(x++ + ++x);

Solve carefully.

Step-by-step:

  1. x++ → 5 (then x becomes 6)
  2. ++x → x becomes 7, value 7
  3. 5 + 7 = 12

Final x = 7

Output:

12

⚠ Interview tip: Avoid writing such confusing code in real projects.

docs.title: Logical AND
docs.text:
if (a > 5 && b > 10)

If first condition is false → second NOT evaluated.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
int x = 5;
if (x > 10 && ++x > 6) {
}
System.out.println(x);

Since x > 10 is false → ++x never executes.

Output:

5

docs.title: Difference Between && and &
docs.text:
&& → short-circuit & → evaluates both sides always

Same for:

|| vs |

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Trap
docs.text:
int x = 0;
if (true || ++x > 0) {
}
System.out.println(x);

Output?

true || anything → second not evaluated

Output:

0

docs.title: 3️⃣ Ternary Operator (?:)
docs.text:
Syntax:

condition ? value1 : value2;

Example:

int x = 10;
String result = (x > 5) ? "Big" : "Small";

docs.title: 🔥 Trap
docs.text:
int x = 10;
System.out.println(x > 5 ? x++ : x--);
System.out.println(x);

Step-by-step:

  • condition true
  • prints 10 (post-increment)
  • x becomes 11

Final output:

10
11

docs.title: 4️⃣ Bitwise Operators
docs.text: Used at binary level.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
System.out.println(5 & 3);

Binary:

5 = 0101
3 = 0011
--------------
    0001 = 1

Output:

1

docs.title: Left Shift
docs.text:
System.out.println(5 << 1);

Binary:

0101 << 1 = 1010

Result:

10

Means: 5 * 2

docs.title: Right Shift
docs.text:
System.out.println(8 >> 1);

Binary:

1000 >> 1 = 0100

Result:

4

Means: 8 / 2

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 10;
int y = x++ + ++x + x++;
System.out.println(y);
System.out.println(x);

docs.title: Step-by-Step Execution (Left → Right Evaluation)
docs.text:
Java evaluates operands left to right.

Initial:

x = 10

docs.title: 1️⃣ First term → x++
docs.text:
Post-increment:

  • Use current value → 10
  • Then increment

After this:

Value used = 10
x = 11

docs.title: 2️⃣ Second term → ++x
docs.text:
Pre-increment:

  • Increment first → x = 12
  • Then use value → 12

After this:

Value used = 12
x = 12

docs.title: 3️⃣ Third term → x++
docs.text:
Post-increment:

  • Use current value → 12
  • Then increment

After this:

Value used = 12
x = 13

docs.title: Final Calculation
docs.text:
y = 10 + 12 + 12
y = 34

Final:

x = 13

docs.title: ✅ Output
docs.text:
34
13

docs.title: ⚠ Interview Insight
docs.text:
Interviewers use this to test:

  • Pre vs post increment understanding
  • Left-to-right evaluation
  • Side effects in expressions

And important:

👉 In real projects, never write such code. It reduces readability and increases bugs.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 5;
int y = x++ + x++ + ++x;
System.out.println(y);
System.out.println(x);

docs.title: Step-by-step (Left → Right)
docs.text:
Initial:

x = 5

docs.title: 1️⃣ First term → x++
docs.text:
• Use value → 5
  • Then increment → x becomes 6

Used value = 5 x = 6

docs.title: 2️⃣ Second term → x++
docs.text:
• Use value → 6
  • Then increment → x becomes 7

Used value = 6 x = 7

docs.title: 3️⃣ Third term → ++x
docs.text:
• Increment first → x becomes 8
  • Use value → 8

Used value = 8 x = 8

docs.title: Final Calculation
docs.text:
y = 5 + 6 + 8
y = 19

Final:

x = 8

docs.title: ✅ Output
docs.text:
19
8

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Insight
docs.text:
If interviewer asks:

What is rule for expression evaluation in Java?

Answer:

Java evaluates expressions strictly from left to right. Side effects like post and pre-increment happen during evaluation in that order.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 5;
System.out.println(x++ + x++ + x++);
System.out.println(x);

docs.title: Step-by-step (Left → Right)
docs.text:
Initial:

x = 5

docs.title: 1️⃣ First x++
docs.text:
• Use 5
  • Then x becomes 6

Used = 5 x = 6

docs.title: 2️⃣ Second x++
docs.text:
• Use 6
  • Then x becomes 7

Used = 6 x = 7

docs.title: 3️⃣ Third x++
docs.text:
• Use 7
  • Then x becomes 8

Used = 7 x = 8

docs.title: Final Calculation
docs.text:
5 + 6 + 7 = 18

Final:

x = 8

docs.title: ✅ Output
docs.text:
18
8

docs.title: 🧠 Important Interview Note
docs.text:
Java guarantees:

  • Left-to-right operand evaluation.
  • Post-increment returns old value, then increments.

This is defined behavior (unlike C/C++ where this can be undefined).

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 1;
int y = 1;

if (x++ == 1 && ++y == 2) {
    x++;
}

System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Step-by-Step Execution
docs.text:
Initial:

x = 1
y = 1

docs.title: 1️⃣ Evaluate: x++ == 1
docs.text:
• x++ → use 1
  • then x becomes 2
  • 1 == 1 → true

Now:

x = 2
y = 1

Since first condition is true, second condition will be evaluated (&& needs both true).

docs.title: 2️⃣ Evaluate: ++y == 2
docs.text:
• ++y → y becomes 2
  • 2 == 2 → true

Now:

x = 2
y = 2

Both conditions true → enter if block.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Inside if:
docs.text:
x++;
  • Use 2
  • x becomes 3

Now:

x = 3
y = 2

docs.title: ✅ Final Output
docs.text:
3
2

docs.title: 🧠 What Interviewer Tests Here
docs.text:
• Post vs pre increment
  • Short-circuit &&
  • Evaluation order (left → right)
  • Side effects inside condition

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
```java id="zky2lk" int x = 1; int y = 1;

if (x++ == 2 && ++y == 2) { x++; }

System.out.println(x); System.out.println(y);


---

## Step-by-step

Initial:

* x = 1
* y = 1

### 1️⃣ Evaluate `x++ == 2`

* `x++` uses 1, then x becomes 2
* `1 == 2` → **false**

Now:

* x = 2
* y = 1

### 2️⃣ Short-circuit happens

Because first part of `&&` is false, Java **will not evaluate** `++y == 2`.

So:

* y stays 1
* `if` block not executed

✅ Final output:

* 2
* 1

---

# Code

```java
int x = 10;
int y = 20;

int z = (x > y) ? x++ : y++;
System.out.println(z);
System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Condition:
docs.text:
x > y

10 > 20 → false

So ternary chooses the false branch:

y++

docs.title: Evaluate y++
docs.text:
• Use current value → 20
  • Then increment → y becomes 21

So:

z = 20
y = 21

x was never touched.

docs.title: ✅ Final State
docs.text:
z = 20
x = 10
y = 21

docs.title: 🧠 Important Interview Insight
docs.text:
In ternary operator:

👉 Only ONE branch executes. 👉 The other branch is completely ignored.

Unlike if statements where confusion sometimes happens with side effects, ternary is strictly one branch execution.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 5;
int y = 10;

System.out.println(x > y ? x++ : ++y);
System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Step 1: Evaluate condition
docs.text:
x > y
5 > 10 → false

So ternary chooses false branch:

++y

docs.title: Step 2: Evaluate ++y
docs.text:
Pre-increment:

  • y becomes 11
  • value used = 11

So first print:

11

Now:

x = 5
y = 11

docs.title: Step 3: Print x and y
docs.text:
5
11

docs.title: ✅ Final Output
docs.text:
11
5
11

docs.title: 🧠 What Interviewer Is Testing
docs.text:
• Only one branch executes
  • Pre-increment changes value before usage
  • Order of evaluation

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 3;
int y = 4;

if ((x & 1) == 1 && (y++ > 3)) {
    x++;
}

System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: 1️⃣ Evaluate (x & 1) == 1
docs.text:
• x = 3 → binary 0011
  • 1 → binary 0001
  • 3 & 1 → 0001 → 1

So:

(x & 1) == 1  →  true

Meaning: x is odd (common trick to check odd/even).

docs.title: 2️⃣ Because first part is true, evaluate second part
docs.text:
(y++ > 3)

  • y++ uses current value 4
  • checks 4 > 3 → true
  • then y becomes 5

So the full && becomes:

true && true → true

So if block runs.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Inside if: x++;
docs.text:
x becomes 4.

Final:

  • x = 4
  • y = 5

✅ Output:

4
5

docs.title: 📘 Notes (Important Interview Lines)
docs.text:
• x & 1 checks odd/even:

  • 1 → odd

  • 0 → even
  • && short-circuits, but here it didn’t short-circuit because first condition was true.
  • y++ changes y even if condition is true/false depending on whether that side executes.
int x = 0;
int y = 0;

if (x++ == 0 & y++ == 0) { }
System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Key point: & is NOT short-circuit
docs.text: Even in boolean expressions, single & evaluates both sides.

docs.title: Left side: x++ == 0
docs.text:
• uses x=0 → true
  • then x becomes 1

docs.title: Right side: y++ == 0
docs.text:
• still evaluated because &
  • uses y=0 → true
  • then y becomes 1

So:

  • x = 1
  • y = 1

docs.title: 📘 Notes line (important)
docs.text:
• && short-circuit: may skip right side
  • & evaluates both sides always (even in boolean)

docs.title: 1️⃣ What they do (Interview style)
docs.text:
• << Left shift: shifts bits left, fills right with 0
  • >> Signed right shift: shifts bits right, fills left with sign bit (0 for +, 1 for -)
  • >>> Unsigned right shift: shifts bits right, fills left with 0 always

docs.title: For positive numbers:
docs.text: • >> and >>> behave the same (both fill with 0)

docs.title: For negative numbers:
docs.text:
• >> keeps negative sign (fills 1 on left)
  • >>> makes it “look positive” (fills 0 on left)

docs.title: 5 << 1
docs.text:
5 in binary (8-bit view): 00000101 Shift left 1 → 00001010 = 10

So:

5 << 1  => 10

docs.title: 8 >> 1
docs.text:
8: 00001000 Right shift 1 → 00000100 = 4

So:

8 >> 1  => 4

docs.title: 4️⃣ The BIG Interview Trap: negative numbers
Example: -4 >> 1
docs.text:
Two’s complement (conceptually):

  • -4 ends with bits ...11111100

Signed right shift >> 1 fills with 1 (sign bit):

  • result becomes ...11111110 which is -2

✅ So:

-4 >> 1   => -2

docs.title: Example: -4 >>> 1
docs.text:
Unsigned right shift fills with 0:

  • left side becomes 0-filled
  • result becomes a large positive int

✅ So:

-4 >>> 1  => 2147483646

Why that number?

  • For int (32-bit), -4 is 0xFFFFFFFC
  • >>> 1 → 0x7FFFFFFE = 2147483646

docs.title: 5️⃣ Another common trap: shift count masking
docs.text:
Java masks the shift distance:

  • For int: only lower 5 bits used → n & 31 (0–31)
  • For long: only lower 6 bits used → n & 63 (0–63)

So:

1 << 32   // same as 1 << (32 & 31) = 1 << 0 = 1

This surprises many people.

docs.title: 6️⃣ When shifts act like multiply/divide
docs.text:
• x << 1 ≈ x * 2
  • x >> 1 ≈ x / 2 (for positives)

⚠ For negatives, >> is like divide-by-2 with rounding toward negative infinity behavior depending on bits. Safer to say: “it shifts bits; division equivalence is not always safe for negatives.”

docs.title: ✅ Bitwise shift “How to answer in interview”
docs.text: << shifts left and fills zeros. >> shifts right and preserves the sign bit (arithmetic shift). >>> shifts right and fills zeros (logical shift), so for negative numbers it produces a large positive value.

docs.title: 📘 Operator Precedence Table (Interview-focused)
docs.text:
Highest → Lowest (common ones)

  1. Postfix: expr++ expr-- () [] .
  2. Unary: ++expr --expr + - ! ~ (type)
  3. Multiplicative: * / %
  4. Additive: + -
  5. Shift: << >> >>>
  6. Relational: < <= > >= instanceof
  7. Equality: == !=
  8. Bitwise AND: &
  9. Bitwise XOR: ^
  10. Bitwise OR: |
  11. Logical AND: &&
  12. Logical OR: ||
  13. Ternary: ?:
  14. Assignment: = += -= *= /= &= |= ^= <<= >>= >>>=

docs.title: Golden note:
docs.text: • Same precedence operators evaluate left to right (except assignments are right to left)

docs.title: Trap 1: + vs <<
docs.text:
System.out.println(1 << 2 + 1);

+ happens before << So it becomes 1 << 3 = 8

docs.title: Trap 2: & vs ==
docs.text:
System.out.println(1 & 2 == 0);

== happens before &? Actually: & has lower precedence than ==, so 2 == 0 evaluated first? Wait carefully:

  • Precedence: Equality (==) is higher than bitwise AND (&) ✅ So expression becomes:
1 & (2 == 0)  // invalid: int & boolean

➡️ This does NOT compile.

Correct way:

System.out.println((1 & 2) == 0); // true

docs.title: ✅ Your Notes Snapshot (copy-paste)
docs.text:
• >> keeps sign bit, >>> fills 0
  • -4 >> 1 = -2
  • -4 >>> 1 = 2147483646
  • int shift distance masked by & 31

docs.title: ✅ Question 1
docs.text:
System.out.println(1 << 2 + 1);

You answered 8 ✔ Correct.

Because:

  • 2 + 1 = 3
  • 1 << 3 = 8

docs.title: ✅ Question 2
docs.text:
System.out.println(-1 >>> 1);

Let me explain quickly and finish this topic cleanly.

  • -1 in 32-bit binary = all 1s

11111111 11111111 11111111 11111111

  • >>> 1 shifts right and fills left with 0

So result becomes:

01111111 11111111 11111111 11111111

That equals:

2147483647

So answer is:

2147483647

docs.title: ✅ Operators Topic – Completed
docs.text:
You now clearly understand:

  • Pre vs Post increment
  • Short-circuit vs &
  • Ternary
  • Bitwise basics
  • Shift traps
  • Precedence basics

That’s strong.

docs.title: 📘 CONTROL FLOW IN JAVA
docs.text:
We’ll cover:

1️⃣ if / else 2️⃣ switch 3️⃣ Loops (for, while, do-while) 4️⃣ break, continue 5️⃣ Common Interview Traps

docs.title: Basic
docs.text:
if (condition) {
   // code
}

Condition must be boolean.

⚠ Java does NOT allow:

if (1)   // ❌ Not allowed (C allows this)

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Trap 1
docs.text:
int x = 5;
if (x = 10) { }  // ❌

Why error?

Because:

  • x = 10 returns int
  • if expects boolean

In C it works. In Java it doesn’t.

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Trap 2: No Braces
docs.text:
int x = 5;
if (x > 3)
    System.out.println("Hi");
    System.out.println("Hello");

Output:

Hi
Hello

Because only first statement is inside if.

Always use {} in real projects.

docs.title: Basic
docs.text:
int x = 2;

switch(x) {
    case 1:
        System.out.println("One");
        break;
    case 2:
        System.out.println("Two");
        break;
}

docs.title: 🔥 Trap: Fall-through
docs.text:
int x = 1;

switch(x) {
    case 1:
        System.out.println("One");
    case 2:
        System.out.println("Two");
}

Output:

One
Two

Because no break.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap: Valid Types
docs.text:
Switch supports:

  • byte
  • short
  • char
  • int
  • String
  • enum

❌ Not allowed:

  • long
  • float
  • double
  • boolean

docs.title: 🔥 Trap: Duplicate case
docs.text:
final int x = 1;
switch(1) {
    case x:  // allowed (compile-time constant)
}

But:

int x = 1;
case x;  // ❌ not constant

docs.title: FOR Loop
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
   System.out.println(i);
}

docs.title: 🔥 Trap
docs.text:
for(;;) {
   System.out.println("Hi");
}

Infinite loop.

All 3 parts optional.

docs.title: WHILE
docs.text:
while(condition) {
}

docs.title: DO-WHILE
docs.text:
Runs at least once.

do {
   System.out.println("Hi");
} while(false);

Output:

Hi

docs.title: Break
docs.text: Stops loop.

docs.title: Continue
docs.text: Skips current iteration.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    if(i == 2) continue;
    System.out.print(i);
}

Output:

0134

docs.title: 5️⃣ Labelled Break (Interview Trap)
docs.text:
outer:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
        break outer;
    }
}

Exits both loops.

docs.title: ✅ Question 1 – Dangling Else
docs.text:
int x = 0;
if (x > 0)
    if (x < 10)
        System.out.println("A");
    else
        System.out.println("B");

docs.title: Rule:
docs.text:
👉 In Java, else always matches the nearest unmatched if.

So else belongs to:

if (x < 10)

This is called the Dangling Else Problem.

In real code → always use braces {}.

docs.title: ✅ Question 2 – While with Semicolon
docs.text:
int x = 0;
while(x++ < 3);
System.out.println(x);

Notice:

while(x++ < 3);   // <-- semicolon

This is an empty loop.

Step-by-step:

  • x=0 → 0<3 → true → x=1
  • x=1 → 1<3 → true → x=2
  • x=2 → 2<3 → true → x=3
  • x=3 → 3<3 → false → x=4 (because post-increment happens)

Loop exits.

Final x = 4

Correct answer: 4

docs.title: ✅ Question 3 – For Loop with Print in Update
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; System.out.print(i++));

Execution order in for:

Initialization
Condition
Body
Update

But body is empty because of semicolon.

So:

  • i=0 → condition true → update prints 0 (i becomes 1)
  • i=1 → condition true → update prints 1 (i becomes 2)
  • i=2 → condition true → update prints 2 (i becomes 3)
  • i=3 → condition false → stop

Output:

012

Correct.

docs.title: 🧠 You Are Strong in Control Flow
docs.text:
You are thinking clearly about:

  • Semicolon traps
  • Post increment behavior
  • Loop evaluation order
  • Dangling else

This is very good sign.

docs.title: ✅ Question 4 – Dangling Else
docs.text:
int x = 0;
if (x > 0)
    if (x < 10)
        System.out.println("A");
    else
        System.out.println("B");

docs.title: Rule:
docs.text:
👉 In Java, else always matches the nearest unmatched if.

So else belongs to:

if (x < 10)

This is called the Dangling Else Problem.

In real code → always use braces {}.

docs.title: ✅ Question 5 – While with Semicolon
docs.text:
int x = 0;
while(x++ < 3);
System.out.println(x);

Notice:

while(x++ < 3);   // <-- semicolon

This is an empty loop.

Step-by-step:

  • x=0 → 0<3 → true → x=1
  • x=1 → 1<3 → true → x=2
  • x=2 → 2<3 → true → x=3
  • x=3 → 3<3 → false → x=4 (because post-increment happens)

Loop exits.

Final x = 4

Correct answer: 4

docs.title: ✅ Question 6 – For Loop with Print in Update
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; System.out.print(i++));

Execution order in for:

Initialization
Condition
Body
Update

But body is empty because of semicolon.

So:

  • i=0 → condition true → update prints 0 (i becomes 1)
  • i=1 → condition true → update prints 1 (i becomes 2)
  • i=2 → condition true → update prints 2 (i becomes 3)
  • i=3 → condition false → stop

Output:

012

Correct.

docs.title: 🧠 You Are Strong in Control Flow
docs.text:
You are thinking clearly about:

  • Semicolon traps
  • Post increment behavior
  • Loop evaluation order
  • Dangling else

This is very good sign.

docs.title: ✅ Next: Nested Loops (Very Important)
docs.text:
Interviewers use nested loops to test:

  • Understanding of iteration
  • Dry run ability
  • Time complexity thinking
  • Pattern logic

docs.title: 📘 1️⃣ Basic Nested Loop
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
        System.out.print(i + "" + j + " ");
    }
}

docs.title: 📘 2️⃣ Classic Pattern Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
    System.out.println();
}

Think step-by-step.

docs.title: 📘 3️⃣ Time Complexity Insight
docs.text:
Nested loops like:

for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for(int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
    }
}

Time complexity = O(n²)

But this:

for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for(int j = 0; j < i; j++) {
    }
}

Time complexity = 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²) (still quadratic)

Interviewers love asking this.

Now let’s quickly verify like interview dry-run style.

docs.title: ✅ Pattern 1
docs.text:
for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
    System.out.println();
}

i = 1 → print 1 star i = 2 → print 2 stars i = 3 → print 3 stars

So:

*
**
***

Correct.

docs.title: ✅ Pattern 2
docs.text:
for(int i = 3; i >= 1; i--) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
        System.out.print(j);
    }
    System.out.println();
}

i = 3 → 1 2 3 i = 2 → 1 2 i = 1 → 1

So:

123
12
1

Correct.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Insight
docs.text:
Nested loops are testing:

  • Control of outer loop
  • Resetting inner loop each iteration
  • Understanding loop boundaries

docs.title: Let’s Confirm (Interview Dry Run)
docs.text:
for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = i; j <= 3; j++) {
        System.out.print(j);
    }
    System.out.println();
}

docs.title: i = 1
docs.text:
j starts at 1 → prints 1 2 3

123

docs.title: i = 2
docs.text:
j starts at 2 → prints 2 3

23

docs.title: i = 3
docs.text:
j starts at 3 → prints 3

3

docs.title: 🧠 Why This Is Important
docs.text:
Interviewers use this to test:

  • Inner loop start value depends on outer loop
  • Changing bounds changes pattern
  • Understanding triangular iteration (used in DSA often)

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
        if(i == j)
            break;
        System.out.print(i + "" + j + " ");
    }
}

docs.title: i = 1
docs.text: • j = 1 → i == j → break (no print)

docs.title: i = 2
docs.text:
• j = 1 → i != j → print 21
  • j = 2 → i == j → break

docs.title: i = 3
docs.text:
• j = 1 → print 31
  • j = 2 → print 32
  • j = 3 → break

✅ Output:

21 31 32

docs.title: Let’s Dry Run (Interview Style)
docs.text:
for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
        if(i == j)
            continue;
        System.out.print(i + "" + j + " ");
    }
}

docs.title: i = 1
docs.text:
• j = 1 → skip (continue)
  • j = 2 → print 12
  • j = 3 → print 13

docs.title: i = 2
docs.text:
• j = 1 → print 21
  • j = 2 → skip
  • j = 3 → print 23

docs.title: i = 3
docs.text:
• j = 1 → print 31
  • j = 2 → print 32
  • j = 3 → skip

✅ Final Output:

12 13 21 23 31 32

docs.title: 🧠 Key Difference (Interview Gold)
docs.text: break continue exits loop skips current iteration stops inner loop completely moves to next iteration

docs.title: 📘 MODERN SWITCH
docs.text:
Now we move to Switch Advanced + Modern Switch (Java 14+)

This topic is important for interviews because many candidates only know old switch.

docs.title: 📘 1️⃣ Traditional Switch (Old Style)
docs.text:
Basic syntax:

switch(expression) {
    case value1:
        // code
        break;
    case value2:
        // code
        break;
    default:
        // code
}

docs.title: 1️⃣ Expression Type Must Be:
docs.text:
Allowed:

  • byte
  • short
  • char
  • int
  • String
  • enum

❌ Not allowed:

  • long
  • float
  • double
  • boolean

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 1: Fall-Through
docs.text:
int x = 2;

switch(x) {
    case 1:
        System.out.println("One");
    case 2:
        System.out.println("Two");
    case 3:
        System.out.println("Three");
}

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Two
Three

Because no break.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 2: Order of Cases
docs.text:
Cases can be in any order.

switch(x) {
    case 5:
    case 1:
    case 3:
}

Valid.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 3: Case Must Be Compile-Time Constant
docs.text:
int a = 5;
switch(5) {
    case a:   // ❌ ERROR
}

But:

final int a = 5;
case a:   // ✅ allowed

Because final primitive constant is compile-time constant.

docs.title: 📘 2️⃣ Switch with String
docs.text:
String day = "MON";

switch(day) {
    case "MON":
        System.out.println("Start");
        break;
}

Internally, Java uses hashcode comparison.

docs.title: 📘 3️⃣ Modern Switch (Java 14+)
docs.text: This is very important for modern interviews.

docs.title: ✅ Switch Expression (Arrow Style)
docs.text:
int day = 2;

String result = switch(day) {
    case 1 -> "Monday";
    case 2 -> "Tuesday";
    default -> "Other";
};

System.out.println(result);

No break needed.

docs.title: 🔥 Key Difference
docs.text:
Old switch:

  • statement
  • needs break
  • fall-through possible

New switch:

  • expression
  • no fall-through
  • returns value
  • cleaner syntax

docs.title: 📘 4️⃣ Multiple Cases in One Line (Modern)
docs.text:
int day = 2;

String result = switch(day) {
    case 1, 2, 3 -> "Weekday";
    case 6, 7 -> "Weekend";
    default -> "Other";
};

docs.title: 📘 5️⃣ Block Style in Modern Switch
docs.text:
int day = 2;

String result = switch(day) {
    case 2 -> {
        System.out.println("Processing...");
        yield "Tuesday";
    }
    default -> "Other";
};

yield is required when using block.

docs.title: ✅ Question 1
docs.text:
int x = 1;

switch(x) {
    case 1:
        System.out.println("A");
    default:
        System.out.println("B");
    case 2:
        System.out.println("C");
}

docs.title: Execution:
docs.text:
• x = 1 → case 1 matches
  • Print A
  • No break → fall through
  • default → print B
  • case 2 → print C

✅ Output:

A
B
C

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
int x = 1;

String result = switch(x) {
    case 1 -> "A";
    case 2 -> "B";
};

System.out.println(result);

docs.title: The Real Reason
docs.text:
Modern switch (switch expression) must be exhaustive.

That means:

  • It must cover all possible values
  • Or have a default

Since int can be many values (not just 1 and 2), compiler says:

"Switch expression does not cover all possible input values"

So it does NOT compile.

docs.title: Correct Version
docs.text:
String result = switch(x) {
    case 1 -> "A";
    case 2 -> "B";
    default -> "Other";
};

Now it compiles.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Insight
docs.text:
Old switch:

  • default optional

New switch expression:

  • must be exhaustive
  • especially for int

BUT:

If switching on enum and covering all enum constants, default is not required.

Example:

enum Day { MON, TUE }

String result = switch(day) {
    case MON -> "Start";
    case TUE -> "Next";
};

This compiles because enum values are fully covered.

docs.title: 🧠 Important Interview Insight
docs.text:
Modern switch:

  • Multiple values per case allowed (case 1, 2)
  • No fall-through
  • Must be exhaustive
  • Cleaner and safer than old switch

docs.title: ✅ Control Flow Topic Status
docs.text:
You now understand:

  • if / else (dangling else)
  • loops (semicolon traps)
  • nested loops
  • break vs continue
  • traditional switch
  • modern switch expression
  • exhaustiveness rule

That is strong foundation.

docs.title: 📘 METHODS & STACK FRAMES
docs.text:
We will cover:

1️⃣ What happens when a method is called 2️⃣ Stack memory structure 3️⃣ Stack Frame creation 4️⃣ Pass-by-value (big confusion area) 5️⃣ Recursion stack behavior 6️⃣ Interview traps

docs.title: 1️⃣ What Happens When a Method is Called?
docs.text:
Example:

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 5;
        int result = add(x);
        System.out.println(result);
    }

    static int add(int num) {
        return num + 10;
    }
}

docs.title: 🧠 Step 1: Program starts
docs.text:
• JVM creates main thread
  • Stack created for main thread
  • main() stack frame pushed

docs.title: 🧠 Step 2: x = 5
docs.text:
main()
  x = 5

docs.title: 🧠 Step 3: add(x) is called
docs.text:
• New stack frame created for add()
  • Parameter num receives copy of x

Important: 👉 Java is pass-by-value 👉 num gets copy of 5

docs.title: 🧠 Step 4: return num + 10
docs.text:
• 15 returned
  • add() frame destroyed (popped)

docs.title: 🧠 Step 5: Print result
docs.text:
Output:

15

docs.title: 2️⃣ What is a Stack Frame?
docs.text:
Every method call creates:

  • Local variables
  • Parameters
  • Return address
  • Operand stack

Stored inside a stack frame.

When method finishes: 👉 Frame is popped 👉 Memory automatically cleaned

docs.title: 3️⃣ Important Rule
docs.text:
Each thread has:

  • Its own stack
  • Multiple stack frames

Stack is LIFO (Last In First Out).

docs.title: 4️⃣ Big Interview Question: Is Java Pass-by-Reference?
docs.text:
Answer:

❌ NO ✔ Java is always pass-by-value

Even for objects.

Example:

static void change(int x) {
    x = 100;
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int a = 10;
    change(a);
    System.out.println(a);
}

Output:

10

Because:

  • Copy of 10 passed
  • Original not changed

docs.title: 5️⃣ Object Pass-by-Value Trap
docs.text:
class Person {
    int age;
}

static void change(Person p) {
    p.age = 50;
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    Person obj = new Person();
    obj.age = 20;
    change(obj);
    System.out.println(obj.age);
}

Output:

50

Why?

Because:

  • Copy of reference passed
  • Both point to same object
  • Object modified

But if:

static void change(Person p) {
    p = new Person();
    p.age = 100;
}

Then output:

20

Because:

  • Only local copy changed
  • Original reference unchanged

docs.title: 🔥 Now I Test Your Understanding
docs.text:
Predict output:

static void modify(int a) {
    a = a + 5;
}

static void modifyArray(int[] arr) {
    arr[0] = 100;
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int x = 10;
    modify(x);
    System.out.println(x);

    int[] nums = {1, 2, 3};
    modifyArray(nums);
    System.out.println(nums[0]);
}

✅ Output:

10
100

Now the interview-level explanation (short but deep):

docs.title: 1️⃣ modify(x)
docs.text:
• x is a primitive (int)
  • Java passes a copy of the value (10) into a
  • Inside method, a becomes 15, but that’s only the local copy
  • x in main stays 10

So first print: 10

docs.title: 2️⃣ modifyArray(nums)
docs.text:
• nums is a reference variable
  • Java passes a copy of the reference
  • Both arr (inside method) and nums (in main) point to the same array object in heap
  • Changing arr[0] changes the same heap array

So second print: 100

docs.title: 📘 Notes Line (Most Important)
docs.text:
✅ Java is pass-by-value always.

  • For primitives: value copy
  • For objects/arrays: reference copy (so object can be mutated)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Person p = new Person(20);

changeRef(p);
System.out.println(p.age);

changeObj(p);
System.out.println(p.age);

docs.title: 1️⃣ changeRef(p)
docs.text:
static void changeRef(Person p) {
    p = new Person(99);
}

docs.title: What happens?
docs.text:
• A copy of reference is passed
  • Inside method, p now points to a NEW object
  • But original reference in main still points to old object

So after method:

main → p → Person(age=20)

Output:

20

docs.title: 2️⃣ changeObj(p)
docs.text:
static void changeObj(Person p) {
    p.age = 99;
}

docs.title: What happens?
docs.text:
• Copy of reference passed
  • Both references point to SAME object
  • Modifying p.age modifies heap object

So:

main → p → Person(age=99)

Output:

99

docs.title: 🧠 Interview-Level Explanation (Memorize This)
docs.text:
Java is strictly pass-by-value. When passing objects, the value passed is a copy of the reference. Therefore, methods can modify the object’s state but cannot change the original reference.

This line alone can impress interviewers.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Stack Frame Deep Test
docs.text:
Predict output:

static void fun(int x) {
    x = x + 1;
    System.out.println(x);
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int x = 5;
    fun(x);
    System.out.println(x);
}

✅ Correct Output:

6
5

docs.title: Why (quick stack-frame reasoning)
docs.text:
• fun(x) receives a copy of x (5)
  • inside fun, local x becomes 6 → prints 6
  • main’s x stays 5 → prints 5

docs.title: 📘 Stack Frames + Recursion (Next)
docs.text: Now we move to recursion because it’s the clearest way to see stack frames.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
static void f(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return;
    System.out.print(n + " ");
    f(n - 1);
    System.out.print(n + " ");
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    f(3);
}

✅ Correct Output:

3 2 1 1 2 3

Now let’s make it crystal clear with stack-frame thinking (this is what interviewers love).

docs.title: Why this output happens
docs.text:
f(3):
 print 3
  f(2):
   print 2
    f(1):
     print 1
      f(0): return
     print 1   // after returning
   print 2
 print 3

So:

  • First half prints while going down the recursion: 3 2 1
  • Second half prints while coming back up: 1 2 3

docs.title: 📘 Notes (One line)
docs.text:
Recursion has two phases:

  • Forward (call) phase
  • Backtracking (return) phase

docs.title: 🔥 Next Recursion + Stack Frame Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

static int g(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    return n + g(n - 1);
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    System.out.println(g(3));
}

✅ Correct Output.

6

g(3) computes:

  • g(3) = 3 + g(2)
  • g(2) = 2 + g(1)
  • g(1) = 1 + g(0)
  • g(0) = 0

So total = 3 + 2 + 1 + 0 = 6.

docs.title: 🔥 Next: Stack Overflow + Recursion Depth (Interview)
docs.text:
If interviewer asks:

“Why does recursion cause StackOverflowError?”

You answer:

Each recursive call creates a new stack frame. If recursion is too deep (or has no base case), the call stack grows until it exceeds the stack memory limit, causing StackOverflowError.

docs.title: Final Method + Stack Frame Trap (Super Common)
docs.text:
Predict output:

static void h(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return;
    h(n - 1);
    System.out.print(n + " ");
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    h(3);
}

✅ Correct Output:

1 2 3

Reason (quick, interview-style):

  • h(3) calls h(2) calls h(1) calls h(0) return
  • Printing happens after the recursive call returns, so it prints in reverse order while the stack unwinds: 1 2 3

docs.title: ✅ Methods & Stack Frames Notes — Completed Core
docs.text:
You now fully understand:

  • Stack vs stack frame
  • Method call frame creation & pop
  • Pass-by-value (primitive + reference)
  • Recursion call stack (forward + backward phase)

docs.title: Definition (Interview Style)
docs.text: Method overloading means defining multiple methods in the same class with the same name but different parameter lists.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class MathUtil {
    int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
}

Same method name → different parameters.

docs.title: Rules for Overloading
docs.text:
Must differ by:

  • Number of parameters
  • Type of parameters
  • Order of parameters

❌ Return type alone cannot differentiate.

Wrong:

int add(int a, int b) { }
double add(int a, int b) { }  // ❌ compile error

docs.title: When Does Overloading Get Resolved?
docs.text:
👉 At compile time

This is called:

Compile-time polymorphism

docs.title: 🔥 Overloading Interview Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Test {
    void show(int a) {
        System.out.println("int");
    }

    void show(long a) {
        System.out.println("long");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Test t = new Test();
        t.show(10);
    }
}

What prints?

Answer: int

Because:

  • 10 is int literal
  • Exact match preferred

docs.title: Definition (Interview Style)
docs.text: Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Animal {
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal sound");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}

docs.title: When Is Overriding Resolved?
docs.text:
👉 At runtime

This is called:

Runtime polymorphism Dynamic method dispatch

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Animal a = new Dog();
a.sound();

Output:

Dog barks

Because:

  • Method call decided at runtime
  • Based on object type (Dog)

docs.title: 🔥 Overriding Rules
docs.text:
• Method signature must be same
  • Cannot reduce access modifier
  • Return type must be same or covariant
  • Cannot override static method
  • Cannot override final method
  • Private methods are not overridden

docs.title: 🔥 Very Common Interview Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Parent {
    static void show() {
        System.out.println("Parent");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    static void show() {
        System.out.println("Child");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Parent p = new Child();
        p.show();
    }
}

✅ Output:

Parent

Now let’s deeply understand why — this is a very common interview trap.

docs.title: Why It Prints "Parent"
docs.text:
Parent p = new Child();
p.show();

Key point:

👉 show() is static

Static methods:

  • Belong to the class
  • Not to the object
  • Are resolved at compile time
  • Are not overridden — they are hidden

This is called:

Method Hiding

docs.title: What happens internally?
docs.text:
Compiler looks at:

Parent p

Since reference type is Parent, compiler binds method call to:

Parent.show();

It does NOT check the object type (new Child()).

So output:

Parent

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Answer Line
docs.text:
Static methods are not overridden. They are hidden. Method call is resolved at compile time based on reference type.

That one sentence can impress interviewer.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Slightly Harder Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Parent {
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Parent");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    @Override
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Child");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Parent p = new Child();
        p.show();
    }
}

✅ Correct.

Child

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
Because this time show() is not static.

Parent p = new Child();
p.show();

For non-static methods:

  • Java uses dynamic method dispatch
